mnemonics.title: Cyclic Shift Property
mnemonics.content: Cyclic codes cycle: Shift any codeword and you get another one. Think of a circle where positions rotate but stay in the code!

mnemonics.title: Generator Polynomial Degree
mnemonics.content: g(x) degree = parity bits = n - k. Remember: 'g' for gaps (parity bits that fill the gaps between message bits)

mnemonics.title: Syndrome = Remainder
mnemonics.content: Syndrome s(x) = r(x) mod h(x). Think: syndrome 'S'eeks 'R'emainder 'M'odulo 'H'. S = R mod H!

mnemonics.title: Error Correction Formula
mnemonics.content: t = floor((d-1)/2). Remember: 't' errors need 'd'istance at least 2t+1. Think of 't' as 'twin' of 'd' minus one!

mnemonics.title: x^n - 1 Factorization
mnemonics.content: x^n - 1 = g(x) × h(x). Think: generator and parity CHECK multiply to give the 'whole' n-cycle!

mnemonics.title: CRC Polynomial Use
mnemonics.content: CRC uses cyclic codes for error detection. The remainder of division gives the check bits - if remainder=0, no error detected!

acronyms.usage: Error detection using cyclic codes in data transmission, storage (ZIP, Ethernet, PNG)
Class of cyclic codes with designed distance for multiple error correction
Hardware implementation of cyclic code encoding and syndrome computation
Finite field used for cyclic code symbol representation, typically GF(2)
In context: HSF approach - High-speed feedback decoding for cyclic codes

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circle with n positions. A codeword written around the circle can rotate - any rotation gives another valid codeword. Draw a circle with bits 1011, then rotate clockwise to get 1101, 1110, 0111.
Think of polynomial long division like number division. g(x) = divisor, message × x^r = dividend, remainder = parity bits. Use the standard division algorithm but with XOR instead of subtraction.

Draw boxes (flip-flops) in a row. Where g(x) has coefficient 1, draw feedback line. For g(x)=1+x+x^3, connect flip-flops at positions with feedback taps - shows how shift register implements polynomial division.
Show syndrome as 'fingerprint' of error. Zero syndrome = no fingerprint = no error. Non-zero syndrome = unique pattern to look up error location - like database lookup for error pattern.
Row 1: coefficients of g(x) padded with zeros. Row 2: shift right by 1. Row 3: shift right by 2. Continue until k rows. Each row is cyclic shift of generator polynomial.

analogies.analogy: Think of cyclic codes like words written in a circle. No matter where you start reading (clockwise), you get meaningful words - the codeword cycles around!
g(x) is like a DNA strand - it contains all information to generate the entire code family through cyclic shifts, just as DNA creates whole organisms through replication.
Syndrome is like a criminal's fingerprint at a crime scene. Just as police match fingerprint to database, decoder matches syndrome to error pattern table.
Parity check matrix is like a set of inspection rules. Each row checks certain positions. If all rules pass (syndrome=0), the codeword passes inspection.
Minimum distance is like the space between cars on a highway - larger distance (d) provides more buffer to avoid collisions (errors). d=3 means cars must have 2 spaces between them.
LFSR is like an automated division machine - you feed in bits, it shifts and feeds back, automatically computing remainder like long division but much faster in hardware.
